issues.original: In most classrooms, determinants are introduced as a formula to memorize: You plug in numbers, compute, and move on. That misses the point entirely.
issues.fixed: In most classrooms, determinants are introduced as a formula to memorize: you plug in numbers, compute, and move on. This misses the point entirely.

issues.original: Once you internalize this, every property of determinants stops being a rule to memorize and starts being something you can *see*. The product rule [FORMULA] becomes obvious — two scalings compose multiplicatively. [FORMULA] means space gets crushed flat. [FORMULA] says the inverse must undo the scaling. The sign of the determinant tells you whether orientation was preserved or flipped.
issues.fixed: Once you internalize this, every property of determinants stops being a rule to memorize and starts being something you can *see*. The product rule [FORMULA] becomes obvious — two scalings multiply. [FORMULA] means space gets crushed flat. [FORMULA] says the inverse must undo the scaling. The sign of the determinant tells you whether orientation is preserved or flipped.

issues.original: The unit square (area [FORMULA]) becomes a parallelogram of area [FORMULA]. *Every* shape in the plane is rescaled by the same factor [FORMULA] — a circle of area [FORMULA] becomes an ellipse of area [FORMULA], a triangle of area [FORMULA] becomes a triangle of area [FORMULA], and so on. The matrix does not care about the shape, only about the local area element.

The matrix cares only about the local area element, not the shape.

issues.original: Set the photocopier to '200%': Width doubles, height doubles, but **area quadruples** (not doubles). The determinant gives the area scaling directly, and that '[FORMULA]' is exactly the surprise built into linear maps.
issues.fixed: Set the photocopier to '200%': width doubles, height doubles, but **area quadruples** (not doubles). The determinant gives the area scaling directly, and that '[FORMULA]' is exactly the surprise in linear maps.

issues.original: To build intuition, look at three different [FORMULA]'s acting on the unit square: - **Shear**, [FORMULA]: the parallelogram leans, but its area is unchanged. (Imagine pushing the top of a stack of books sideways — the volume of the stack does not change.) - **Stretch**, [FORMULA]: one direction is doubled; area doubles. - **Compression**, [FORMULA]: one direction is halved; area is halved.
issues.fixed: To build intuition, look at three different [FORMULA]'s acting on the unit square: - **Shear**, [FORMULA]: the parallelogram leans, but its area remains unchanged. (Imagine pushing the top of a stack of books sideways — the volume does not change.) - **Stretch**, [FORMULA]: one direction is doubled; area doubles. - **Compression**, [FORMULA]: one direction is halved; area is halved.

issues.original: Take a right-hand glove. Rotate it, stretch it, squash it — it stays a right-hand glove. But turn it inside out, and it becomes a left-hand glove. That 'inside-out' operation is exactly the kind of transformation a negative determinant performs in our model. Rotations and stretches keep [FORMULA]; reflections flip the sign.
issues.fixed: Take a right-hand glove. Rotate it, stretch it, or squash it — it stays a right-hand glove. But turn it inside out, and it becomes a left-hand glove. That 'inside-out' operation is exactly what a negative determinant does in our model. Rotations and stretches keep [FORMULA]; reflections flip the sign.

issues.original: This gives one of the cleanest equivalences in linear algebra: It also gives a fast practical test for linear dependence: just compute the determinant.
issues.fixed: This gives one of the cleanest equivalences in linear algebra. It also provides a fast practical test for linear dependence: just compute the determinant.

issues.original: If you've used PyTorch or TensorFlow, you've met the word "tensor" hundreds of times. PyTorch calls every array [CODE]; TensorFlow puts it in the product name. But what *is* a tensor, and why did frameworks borrow this physics-flavored word for what looks like a multi-dimensional array?
issues.fixed: If you've used PyTorch or TensorFlow, you've seen the word "tensor" hundreds of times. PyTorch calls every array [CODE]; TensorFlow includes it in the product name. But what is a tensor, and why do these frameworks use this physics-flavored term for what seems like a multi-dimensional array?

issues.original: A **tensor** is a generalization of vectors and matrices to arbitrary dimensions. An [FORMULA]th-order tensor is an [FORMULA]-dimensional array, denoted [FORMULA], where [FORMULA] are the sizes of each axis.

issues.original: To work with a tensor, you need ways to "see into" it. Two essential views are **fibers** and **slices**.
issues.fixed: To work with a tensor, you need ways to "see into" it. Two essential views are **fibers** and **slices**.

issues.original: Picture a Rubik's cube (a 3rd-order tensor). Push a toothpick straight through it along one direction. The string of small cubes you pierce is a **fiber** — a 1-D slice through the tensor.
issues.fixed: Imagine a Rubik's cube (a 3rd-order tensor). Push a toothpick straight through it along one direction. The string of small cubes you pierce is a **fiber** — a 1-D slice through the tensor.
